region_of_origin: India (Mughal Empire origin); widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Spiced basmati rice cooked with marinated mutton

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic biryani concept but no specific Mutton Biryani entry. Nearest parent is a spiced rice dish with red meat. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for mutton biryani.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite rice dishes — spiced rice with mutton (biryani style) (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for mutton biryani; A03SL (composite rice dishes) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept exists for mutton biryani at this time.

main_ingredients: whole spices (star anise, cloves, mace, cardamom, cinnamon)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of mutton biryani weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a generous mound of saffron-layered basmati rice with bone-in mutton pieces, fried onions, and fresh herb garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Food Code EI000047
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN food composition database for Mutton Biryani (Food Code EI000047). Core macros and most micronutrients are verified at confidence 1.0; copper, manganese, and vitamin K are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — basmati rice (cooked); applied as analogous basis for mutton biryani
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked basmati rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate component of mutton biryani. The high fat (8.2 g/100 g) and protein (10.5 g/100 g) content from mutton and ghee moderates gastric emptying, supporting a low-to-medium GI classification. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: (55 × 80.1 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 44.1. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for mutton biryani was found in the University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 2016 mg per 450 g serving, representing ~88% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit mutton biryani to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single 450 g serving provides approximately 2016 mg sodium, close to the entire daily recommended sodium ceiling of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 55, basmati rice basis) with high glycemic load per serving (~44) due to large portion size
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume mutton biryani in moderation; the basmati rice GI of ~55 is more favourable than jasmine rice, and the fat and protein from mutton slow gastric emptying, but the total carbohydrate load of ~80 g per 450 g serving requires careful portion control and blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g; ~15.75 g per serving) and cholesterol (55 mg/100 g; ~247.5 mg per serving) from mutton and ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit mutton biryani to occasional consumption, as a 450 g serving delivers approximately 15.75 g saturated fat and 247.5 mg dietary cholesterol, both of which can adversely affect LDL cholesterol levels when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid in mutton may promote pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways at high intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume mutton biryani in moderation; while the biryani spice blend (turmeric, ginger, cloves) provides anti-inflammatory phytochemicals, the arachidonic acid content of mutton can exacerbate inflammation if consumed frequently in large portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Biryani is a rich, aromatic South Asian rice dish of Mughal origin, widely celebrated across India and popular in Singapore's Indian Muslim restaurant scene. It is prepared by marinating bone-in mutton (goat meat) in yoghurt, biryani masala, ginger-garlic paste, and caramelised onions, then slow-cooking it using the dum method — a sealed-pot technique in which partially cooked saffron-infused basmati rice is layered over the spiced mutton and steamed under pressure so that the rice absorbs the fragrant mutton juices. The finished dish is characterised by its dual-toned rice (white and golden from saffron), fall-off-the-bone tender meat, and a deeply complex spice profile featuring star anise, cloves, mace, cardamom, and cinnamon. Nutritionally, a 450 g restaurant serving provides approximately 810 kcal, 47 g protein, 37 g fat, and 80 g carbohydrate. The dish is a meaningful source of iron (9.9 mg per serving), zinc (14.4 mg), vitamin B12 (8.1 mcg), and phosphorus (684 mg). Sodium is high at ~2016 mg per serving, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The basmati rice GI of ~55 is more favourable than jasmine rice, but the large carbohydrate load requires portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes. Mutton Biryani is halal and gluten-free, and is strongly associated with festive occasions including Hari Raya and Deepavali in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros and most micronutrients are sourced directly from ICMR-NIN (Food Code EI000047) at confidence 1.0. Three minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) are from enriched_json at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked basmati rice as the primary carbohydrate component, which is a well-established and defensible basis.
notes: Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 55 × 80.1 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 44.1. Available carbohydrate = 17.8 g/100 g × 4.5 = 80.1 g. Copper (0.12 mg/100 g), manganese (0.08 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (1.5 mcg/100 g) are from enriched_json source at confidence 0.9 and should be verified against a primary food composition database in a future revision. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by the ICMR-NIN source and are set to -1. Water content is not reported and is set to -1. The GI value of 55 is classified as low (≤55) per the University of Sydney GI classification system; however, the glycemic load of 44.1 per 450 g serving is high, reflecting the large carbohydrate mass. The input record listed gi_level as 'low' which is consistent with GI 55 under the Sydney classification (low ≤55). No zero-macro justification is required as all core macros are non-zero.
